problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem C (the Wordle / Twitter-share dataset, 359 days of NYT Wordle report data). The team had to: (1) explain and predict the daily number of reported Wordle results on Twitter plus the Hard-Mode percentage; (2) predict the full (1,2,3,4,5,6,X) attempt-distribution for a given future word (EERIE, 2023-03-01); (3) classify words by difficulty and relate difficulty to word attributes; (4) describe other interesting features of the dataset. This O-award paper threads all sub-problems through one shared spine: 12 engineered word-attribute indicators that feed every downstream model, giving the solution unusual internal consistency.
models_used: T2PL (Target-2-Players-Lost) model: a custom SIR-style compartmental ODE system that splits the 'Infected' player pool into LOYAL vs GENERAL players with separate decay rates (beta, gamma), plus a lost-return rate theta and a loyalty split (tau, p). Used to fit/forecast daily report counts.

Forward-Euler numerical integration of the ODE system, with parameters (alpha, beta, gamma, T0, L0, tau, theta, p) fit by gradient descent in PyTorch autograd (5000 epochs, lr 0.01) against a rescaled MSE objective (1 - pred/actual)^2.

P&S (Played-and-Shared) model: a Monte-Carlo simulation of a rational Wordle solver (constraint-filter the dictionary on green/yellow/grey feedback, then sample by word frequency), 5000 sims per word, then a 7-parameter share-willingness rescaling W_s1..W_s7 fit by gradient descent to correct for selection bias in who tweets.

K-means clustering (k=3) on the empirical attempt-distributions to define Easy/Medium/Hard labels; compared against Gaussian Mixture Model (EM), selected via Silhouette Coefficient and Calinski-Harabasz index; t-SNE for 2-D visualization.
Random Forest classifier (bagging over trees + feature masking) mapping the 12 word attributes to the 3 difficulty labels (80/20 split, accuracy 0.68).

Multiple linear regression (with previous-day value as a control / lagged variable) to link word attributes -> Hard-Mode %, and dummy-variable regression to link difficulty class -> report count and -> Hard-Mode %.

Feature engineering: 12 indicators across Familiarity (word frequency F), Composition (Nc most-repeated-letter count, Nv vowel count), Association (positional letter frequency LF_i, LF_sum, plus N_FI2 from FP-Growth frequent-itemset mining), Confusion (N_D1/N_D2 = neighbor counts at Levenshtein distance 1/2).
key_techniques: Epidemic-model analogy with a domain-motivated refinement: instead of vanilla SIR, splitting infected into loyal/general subpopulations to capture the sharp Feb spike + slow tail decay that one decay rate could not fit.

Treating ODE parameter estimation as a differentiable optimization problem solved with PyTorch autograd + gradient descent rather than grid search or scipy.curve_fit -- a clever reuse of deep-learning tooling for a 8-parameter dynamical system.

Explicitly modeling sharing/selection bias: recognizing that the simulated solver distribution was systematically slower than reported data (79.6% of sims had higher mean) because successful-fast players over-tweet; correcting it with learned per-score share probabilities (W_s7=0.301 vs W_s1=1.0), cutting error 5.36% -> 1.50%.

Two-stage difficulty pipeline: unsupervised clustering on outcomes to MINT labels, then supervised RF to make those labels predictable from intrinsic word features -- separating 'how hard did it turn out' from 'why'.
Using FP-Growth frequent-itemset mining and Levenshtein-distance neighbor counts as linguistically meaningful difficulty proxies rather than generic NLP embeddings.
Lagged/control-variable regression: putting previous-day Hard-Mode% as a regressor to strip the global trend and isolate the word-attribute fluctuation effect.
Cross-model corroboration as a validity check: EERIE was independently labeled 'hard' both by clustering its P&S-predicted distribution AND by the RF attribute classifier.
Sensitivity analysis via injected rounding noise: multiplying distributions by U(0.95,1.05) and re-clustering; only 3.34% of labels flipped, evidencing robustness.
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on COHERENCE and INTERPRETABILITY, not on exotic math. Three things stand out. (1) A single shared asset -- the 12 indicators -- feeds the dynamic model, the regressions, and the classifier, so the five tasks read as one investigation rather than five disconnected mini-papers; this is exactly the integration MCM judges reward. (2) Every modeling choice is justified by a real-world mechanism the judges can sanity-check: loyal vs general players to explain the two-phase decay, share-willingness to explain why simulation is too pessimistic, Normal-mode non-sharing to explain the counterintuitive 'harder word -> higher Hard-Mode %' result. The team did not just report the weird correlation, they decomposed it (ran extra N-H vs H regressions) and explained it -- judges love a team that chases down an anomaly. (3) It is honest and self-validating: MAPE-based prediction intervals, sensitivity analysis, cross-model agreement on EERIE, and an explicit statement that they kept the model small to avoid overfitting on limited data. The closing letter to the NYT editor turns the analysis into concrete design advice ('pick words hard along only 1-2 indicators, vary which indicator day-to-day for freshness'), closing the loop back to the client's actual question.
section_highlights: The Summary page is a model of the genre: one paragraph per task, each naming the model (T2PL, P&S, K-means+Random Forest, Linear Regression) AND giving the concrete numeric answer (EERIE distribution (0,0,9%,29%,45%,14%,3%), difficulty 'hard'), so a judge knows the deliverables in 60 seconds. The 'Our Work' flowchart (Fig 1) visually shows how indicators feed all later models. Data Preprocessing is unusually strong: they manually corrected 5 misspelled answers by cross-referencing the contest number, flagged the 'nymph' row summing to 126% and DROPPED it rather than rescaling, and corrected the obviously-mistyped 'study' report count (2569 -> 25690) -- concrete data-cleaning that builds trust. The Strengths/Weaknesses section is candid (admits 68% classifier accuracy is mediocre, admits the P&S solver ignores heterogeneous player strategies/vocabularies) and the Further Discussion proposes specific fixes (per-player-type lexicons + optimize the mixture proportions). The hand-written client letter at the end is well-pitched to a non-technical game editor.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The T2PL model fits PLAYERS but the data are TWITTER REPORTS; the paper explicitly assumes (Assumption 4) the two are interchangeable, which is a large, mostly-unjustified leap given that sharing behavior is the very thing they model elsewhere (P&S). This conflation is glossed over.

Random Forest accuracy is only 0.68 with tiny support for the 'hard' class (11 test words), so the per-word difficulty predictions -- including EERIE='hard', precision 0.58 -- are not very reliable; conclusions are stated more confidently than the metric warrants.

Prediction-interval construction is ad hoc: the report-count interval is just point estimate +/- MAPE, and the EERIE distribution interval is 'each point +/- 1.50% error'. These are not statistically grounded intervals (no bootstrap, no propagation of ODE-fit uncertainty).

The P&S simulator assumes every player knows the entire dictionary and plays the same rational filter-then-frequency-sample strategy -- no first-guess strategy, no human vocabulary limits -- the team admits this in Weaknesses but it likely biases the base distribution.

The dynamic-system fit uses gradient descent to a LOCAL minimum from hand-set initial values; no discussion of identifiability of 8 parameters from one report curve, so the loyal/general split numbers (e.g. 'only 19% general by Dec 2022') may be an artifact of the parameterization rather than a discovered fact.

Some figures in this markdown render as placeholder/synthetic tables (e.g. the calendar heatmap shows a suspiciously regular 0.7/0.8/0.9 pattern, the t-SNE scatter looks templated), so a few quantitative visuals cannot be fully trusted from this transcript -- but this is likely an OCR/extraction artifact, not the original paper.

R^2 of the difficulty -> report-count regression is only 0.09, so word difficulty explains very little of the report-count fluctuation; the correction of EERIE's interval by subtracting 2649 rests on a weak relationship.
reusable_patterns: Build ONE shared feature/indicator layer early (here: 12 word attributes) and route every later model through it -- instant cross-task integration that judges reward, and it lets you do correlation/importance analysis that ties everything together.

When a textbook model (SIR) won't fit a two-phase curve, split a compartment by a behaviorally meaningful sub-type (loyal vs casual) with separate rate constants rather than adding opaque terms -- keeps interpretability while gaining flexibility.

Fit ODE / dynamical-system parameters with autograd + gradient descent (PyTorch) against a rescaled relative-error loss; treat the simulator as a differentiable function. Reusable whenever you have a forward model and want to invert for parameters.

When a mechanistic simulation systematically deviates from observed data, don't discard it -- add a small learned correction layer (here 7 share-willingness weights) and fit it; report the before/after error (5.36% -> 1.50%) as evidence the correction is principled, not fudged.

Two-stage difficulty/severity scoring: cluster on OUTCOMES to create labels, then train a supervised model on intrinsic FEATURES to predict those labels -- gives both a definition and an explanation, and the feature-importance/correlation chart becomes a headline result.
Validate predictions by cross-model agreement: predict the same target two independent ways (cluster-the-distribution vs classify-the-attributes) and show they agree (EERIE='hard' both ways).

Robustness via injected noise: perturb inputs by realistic measurement error (U(0.95,1.05) for rounding) and report the % of labels that flip -- a cheap, convincing sensitivity analysis for any clustering/classification result.

Chase anomalies, don't hide them: when a counterintuitive correlation appears (harder word -> more Hard-Mode share), run decomposition regressions (split into Normal vs Hard counts) and give a mechanistic story -- this turns a red flag into a highlight.
Use a lagged dependent variable (previous-day value) as a control regressor to detrend and isolate the effect you actually want to measure.
Close with a client-facing letter that converts statistics into actionable, specific recommendations (e.g. 'make words hard along only 1-2 indicators and rotate which indicator daily for freshness').
